records.ety: [OE. old, ald, AS. ald, eald; akin to D. oud, OS. ald, OFries. ald, old, G. alt, Goth. alpeis, and also to Goth. alan to grow up, Icel. ala to bear, produce, bring up, L. alere to nourish. Cf. Adult, Alderman, Aliment, Auld, Elder.]

defs: Not young; advanced far in years or life; having lived till toward the end of the ordinary term of living; as, an old man; an old age; an old horse; an old tree.
quotes: Let not old age disgrace my high desire.
The melancholy news that we grow old.

defs: Not new or fresh; not recently made or produced; having existed for a long time; as, old wine; an old friendship.
Formerly existing; ancient; not modern; preceding; original; as, an old law; an old custom; an old promise.
Continued in life; advanced in the course of existence; having (a certain) length of existence; -- designating the age of a person or thing; as, an infant a few hours old; a cathedral centuries old.
quotes: And Pharaoh said unto Jacob, How old art thou?
notes: ☞ In this use old regularly follows the noun that designates the age; as, she was eight years old.

defs: Long practiced; hence, skilled; experienced; cunning; as, an old offender; old in vice.
quotes: Vane, young in years, but in sage counsel old.

defs: Long cultivated; as, an old farm; old land, as opposed to new land, that is, to land lately cleared.
Worn out; weakened or exhausted by use; past usefulness; as, old shoes; old clothes.

quotes: If a man were porter of hell gate, he should have old turning the key.

defs: Aged; antiquated; hence, wanting in the mental vigor or other qualities belonging to youth; -- used disparagingly as a term of reproach.
Old-fashioned; wonted; customary; as of old; as, the good old times; hence, colloquially, gay; jolly.
Used colloquially as a term of cordiality and familiarity.
quotes: Refuse profane and old wives' fables.
